title: Validate MLX GPU Cholesky plus batched solve on flagship SPD workflows
description: Turn the existing MLX bridge-backed Cholesky and triangular-solve path into a benchmarked, trustworthy second flagship. Scope: add focused parity tests around am_chol_factor() and am_chol_solve() in fast MLX mode; benchmark representative SPD many-RHS workloads such as ridge-like solves or kernel systems; document where the MLX path is already real and where ArrayFire remains a stub or fallback.
acceptance_criteria: MLX factor and solve paths have benchmark-backed validation; docs describe the Cholesky workflow honestly as implemented and validated, not merely planned; any remaining backend gaps are explicit
notes: Completed with native-MLX parity and benchmark coverage. Added focused fast-MLX package tests for ridge-like and kernel-like SPD many-RHS solves plus backend-local chol smoke coverage. Reliable benchmark invocation on this machine is: Rscript -e "source(\"tools/benchmark-cholesky-runtime.R\", local = TRUE)". Current spot results: ridge_spd 768x768 rhs=64 factor+solve cpu 0.079s vs mlx 0.028s; kernel_spd 640x640 rhs=32 factor+solve cpu 0.042s vs mlx 0.016s. Reconstruction residuals are about 2e-7 and solve errors about 5e-7 versus CPU reference. ArrayFire remains explicit CPU fallback/stub territory for this workflow.

title: Replace src_id transpose shortcut with proper aTransposeView
description: Current state: the shipped resident transpose shortcut keeps t(A) %*% B and A %*% t(B) on the fast resident path by returning a src_id-linked adgeMatrix with transposed host data. That fixes the re-upload problem, but it is still not a true structural view and it pays O(nm) host transpose cost and memory.\n\nGoal: introduce a dedicated Matrix-compatible aTransposeView (or broader aMatrixView family) that carries source linkage and orientation without pretending to be concrete dense storage.
acceptance_criteria:
t(A) on dense matrices returns a dedicated structural view rather than a materialized adgeMatrix
t(A) %*% B and A %*% t(B) still reuse the existing crossprod/tcrossprod resident fast paths
t(t(A)) collapses cleanly without redundant host transposition
as.matrix() or explicit coercion materializes correctly when a concrete dense value is required
The view carries distinct cache and residency identity from the source matrix
notes: This is the real next iteration after the shipped src_id shortcut. It should stay narrow: transpose view only, no scaled views, no deferred expression fusion. It also sets up ALTREP-facing host-materialization work more naturally later.
close_reason: Implemented: aTransposeView S4 class, zero-copy t(), t(t(A)) collapse, %*% dispatch to crossprod/tcrossprod. 882 tests pass.

title: woodbury_solve(): Woodbury identity for O(nk^2) rank-k system updates
Speed up resident t() products via src_id transpose shortcut
description: Current state: resident dense transpose products no longer force a device re-upload. t() on a resident adgeMatrix returns a src_id-linked adgeMatrix with correctly transposed host data, and am_matmul() routes t(A) %*% B and A %*% t(B) through crossprod_resident()/tcrossprod_resident() when possible.\n\nScope of this completed step: ship the narrow fast-path shortcut first, keep semantics correct, and avoid introducing a public lazy algebra DSL.
acceptance_criteria:
Resident t(A) %*% B reuses the source residency through the crossprod resident path
Resident A %*% t(B) reuses the source residency through the tcrossprod resident path
Correctness matches the previous eager materialized behavior
No general deferred expression system is introduced
notes: Implemented as a stepping stone. Follow-up structural-view work should live in a separate open issue rather than mutating this completed shortcut into the final object-model design.
close_reason: Implemented: src_id slot on aMatrix, zero-copy t() for resident matrices, fast-path in am_matmul for t(A)%*%B and A%*%t(B). 5 new tests, 846 pass.

title: Add trace(A) matrix trace
description:
sum(diag(A)) as a named GPU-dispatched function. Currently requires sum(diag(as.matrix(A))) which breaks residency. Needed constantly in REML gradients, matrix calculus, and covariance identities.

Scope:
- trace(A) returns scalar double = sum(diag(A))
- Register S4 setMethod on adgeMatrix
- GPU path: extract diagonal then sum (avoids full materialization for large matrices)
- Tests: matches sum(diag(as.matrix(A))), works on non-square matrices (min(n,p) diagonal)
close_reason: trace(A) added in R/wrappers.R. Scalar sum(diag(A)) with GPU materialization. 957 tests pass.

title: Add explicit am_gemm() public function
description: Expose am_gemm(A, B, C=NULL, alpha=1, beta=1, transA=FALSE, transB=FALSE) as a documented power-user function. Keeps %*% boring and eager (no contract change). AF has native af_gemm; MLX composes mlx_matmul + scaled mlx_add. Files: R/wrappers.R, both backend.R files, NAMESPACE. Tests: am_gemm(A,B) == A %*% B, transA/transB variants, non-NULL C accumulator.
close_reason: Superseded by amatrix-q45 (more detailed spec)

title: Fused am_cov/am_cor: eliminate GPU round-trips in covariance chain
description: Currently center→tcrossprod→scale incurs 2 unnecessary host↔device transfers (centering on CPU, tcrossprod on GPU, scaling on CPU). Add am_cov() and am_cor() that keep the full chain on device. PRD ref: docs/prd.json:611-615. Files: R/wrappers.R (public API), C bridge for fused center+tcrossprod. Target workloads: fMRI, genomics, ridge paths. Expected 2-5x speedup on those workflows.
close_reason: Fused covariance bridge implemented in C (amatrix_mlx_covariance_bridge); am_covariance() dispatches to GPU path eliminating both host materializations. 862 tests pass.

title: Block Lanczos GPU-native truncated SVD (batched GEMM irlba upgrade)
description: Replace the current per-step GEMV Lanczos compatibility path with a GEMM-oriented block Lanczos implementation on MLX after the flagship QR and Cholesky workflows are benchmarked and documented cleanly.
acceptance_criteria: Block Lanczos shows a credible benchmark-backed win over the current am_irlba compatibility path; the work does not pre-empt unfinished flagship workflow hardening
notes: Completed on 2026-04-06 and refined further the same day. Added am_block_lanczos() in R/irlba.R, kept am_block_svd() as the alias surface, and wired am_irlba(..., implementation = 'block') to the new path. Focused coverage lives in tests/testthat/test-block-lanczos.R and the source-driven harnesses live in tools/benchmark-block-lanczos.R and tools/profile-block-lanczos.R. Profiling first identified the repeated MLX resident crossprod transpose as the main loss; the block path now builds a resident-transposed MLX right operator once per solve and uses direct resident matmul helpers on both the left and right products. A second pass then removed unnecessary final global QR work by skipping it whenever the concatenated block bases are already orthonormal within tolerance. Re-verified from source: testthat::test_file('tests/testthat/test-block-lanczos.R') passes. Current benchmark results on this Apple Silicon MLX setup: 1200x600, k=10, block_size=12, n_steps=4 -> irlba::irlba 0.009s, am_irlba 0.219s, am_block_lanczos 0.018s with max_rel_sv_err 0.0468; 3000x1200, k=20, block_size=24, n_steps=4 -> irlba::irlba 0.126s, am_irlba 0.487s, am_block_lanczos 0.091s with max_rel_sv_err 0.0479; 5000x2000, k=20, block_size=24, n_steps=4 -> irlba::irlba 0.421s, am_irlba 0.715s, am_block_lanczos 0.147s with max_rel_sv_err 0.0462. The surface remains explicitly approximate and should not be described as a strict replacement for CPU irlba yet.

title: Add am_rowscale(X, d) and am_colscale(X, d) diagonal scaling without breaking residency
description:
Scale rows or columns of a matrix by a vector without pulling a resident matrix to host. Currently X / d or X * d in base R breaks GPU residency, forcing a round-trip on every call. This is needed for:

- EMMA inner loop: D^{-1} X* = X* scaled by 1/d (row-wise) stays resident for repeated crossprod calls
- Preconditioning: scale rows/cols before a solve
- Standardization: divide columns by standard deviations
- Any diagonal-times-matrix or matrix-times-diagonal op

API:
  am_rowscale(X, d)   # diag(d) %*% X  — scale each row i by d[i]
  am_colscale(X, d)   # X %*% diag(d)  — scale each col j by d[j]

Both return an adgeMatrix with the same backend/precision as X.

Backend:
- CPU fallback: X * d (row) or t(t(X) * d) (col)
- MLX: element-wise multiply keeping the array resident; no host round-trip
- Result should inherit residency from X when possible

Relationship to am_ewise: am_ewise handles scalar and same-shape ops but not broadcast-along-axis. This fills that gap without introducing a general broadcasting DSL.
close_reason: Implemented am_rowscale(X,d) and am_colscale(X,d) in R/wrappers.R. CPU path via host materialisation + rewrap. 25 new tests pass including EMMA rotation pattern test (rowscale + crossprod == crossprod_weighted). GPU-resident path is a future optimisation.

title: Add trace_estim(A, k) stochastic Hutchinson trace estimator
description:
Approximates tr(A) via k Rademacher random probes without materializing A or A^{-1}. Enables REML gradients tr(V^{-1} dV/dθ) at O(nk) cost instead of O(n^3).

Algorithm: E[z' A z] = tr(A) for z ~ Rademacher(±1). Estimate with k probes.
For tr(K^{-1}): supply a solve_fn = function(v) chol_solve(L, v) — probe is z' K^{-1} z.

API:
  trace_estim(A, k = 30, seed = NULL)
  trace_estim(solve_fn, n, k = 30, seed = NULL)  # for implicit A via function

For trace_estim(solve_fn, n, k): generates k random ±1 vectors of length n,
  applies solve_fn to each, computes mean of z' solve_fn(z).

Uses: REML gradient tr(P V_i), log-det approximation, kernel learning.
close_reason: done

title: Symmetric eigendecomposition GPU bridge (mlx_linalg_eigh)
close_reason: done

title: Add dot(x, y) inner product convenience function
description:
Named inner product drop(crossprod(x, y)) as a single function. Currently hot loops require verbose drop(crossprod(...)) or as.double(t(x) %*% y). Needed for GP log-likelihoods, gradient steps, residual norms.

Scope:
- dot(x, y) for vectors: returns scalar double = sum(x * y)
- dot(X, Y) for matrices: returns sum(X * Y) i.e. Frobenius inner product (trace of X'Y)
- GPU path: element-wise multiply then sum, staying resident
- Tests: matches sum(x*y) for vectors, matches sum(X*Y) for matrices, handles adgeMatrix inputs
close_reason: done

title: qr_downdate(): rank-1 Givens-rotation QR update/downdate for incremental fitting and LOO-CV
model cache LRU eviction: max-size and TTL policy to prevent unbounded growth
Add am_quad_form(L, v) quadratic form v'K^{-1}v via Cholesky
description:
Mahalanobis distances, REML score computations, and GP log-likelihoods all require v'K^{-1}v. Composable today via am_chol_solve then a dot product, but an ergonomic wrapper matters when called inside a tight optimisation loop.

Scope:
- am_quad_form(factor, v) where factor is amChol, v is a vector or matrix
- For matrix v: returns t(v) %*% K^{-1} %*% v (p×p result)
- For vector v: returns scalar double
- Internally: z <- am_chol_solve(factor, v); crossprod(v, z)
- Export in NAMESPACE from R/chol-factor.R
- Tests: matches manual solve path, positive result for any nonzero v, batched matrix case
close_reason: Implemented am_quad_form(factor, v) in R/chol-factor.R. Handles vector (scalar result) and matrix (p×p result) inputs. 18 new tests pass.

title: Expose am_eigh() public R wrapper for GPU symmetric eigendecomposition
description:
The mlx_linalg_eigh C bridge was implemented (amatrix-7ze) but there is no public am_eigh() R function. This is the linchpin for EMMA/FaST-LMM style variance component estimation: decompose K = Q Λ Q' once on GPU, then rotate y* = Q'y and X* = Q'X, reducing the LMM to a 1D profile optimisation over delta = sigma2_e/sigma2_b with only diagonal matrix ops in the inner loop.

Scope:
- Add am_eigh(K) to R/wrappers.R dispatching to the MLX eigh bridge (symmetric=TRUE, sorted eigenvalues ascending)
- Export in NAMESPACE; CPU fallback via base::eigen(x, symmetric=TRUE)
- Return named list(values=, vectors=) consistent with base::eigen
- Register setMethod eigen on adgeMatrix for the symmetric case
- Tests: eigenvalue reconstruction K ≈ Q diag(λ) Q', orthogonality Q'Q ≈ I, fast-mode MLX dispatch
close_reason: Implemented am_eigh() in R/wrappers.R as thin wrapper over am_eigen(symmetric=TRUE); MLX backend already dispatches to amatrix_mlx_eigh. 18 new tests pass.

title: Add lu_factor() / lu_solve() cached LU factorization for non-SPD systems
description:
Mirrors chol_factor/chol_solve but for general (non-SPD) square matrices. Allows caching the LU factorization across multiple RHS solves, e.g. when solving (X'X + lambda*I) for many lambda values where the matrix is not guaranteed SPD.

Scope:
- lu_factor(A) returns an amLU object caching the factorization
- lu_solve(factor, B) solves A x = B for one or many RHS columns
- amLU S4 class with slots: factor (list with L, U, pivot), source_id, precision, backend
- Cache keyed by object_id (same pattern as chol_factor)
- CPU fallback via base::solve(); MLX backend via existing solve bridge
- Tests: lu_solve matches solve(A, B), cached vs uncached identical, non-square error

title: Reduce compact MLX QR cold factor-build cost
description: Keep pushing the flagship many-RHS QR workflow where the current bottleneck actually is: cold compact-factor construction, not the hot solve path. Scope: profile compact QR factor build separately from qr.coef()/many_lm hot helpers; move more compact-path setup below R if the cost is bridge/setup dominated; re-benchmark resident-Q versus compact MLX paths on many_lm.
acceptance_criteria: Compact QR cold-build cost is measured and reduced or clearly explained; docs can state whether compact or resident-Q is the premium QR implementation today; the flagship many-RHS benchmark story is stronger after the pass
notes: Completed on 2026-04-06. Removed eager host compact-factor construction from the MLX QR boundary: native resident QR now keeps fallback compact factors reconstructable on demand, and non-TSQR compact MLX QR now keeps a lazy host compact factor that materializes only when a compact helper needs it. Source-loaded verification: main conformance and backend-registration slices pass. Spot benchmarks show 768x256 non-TSQR qr(x) cold build now lands around 0.004 s for both native and compact modes with compact factors still lazy; on 4096x128 rhs=128 the remaining compact cold cost is the TSQR build itself, with resident-Q still the premium QR path today.

title: Add kron(A, B) Kronecker product
description:
Kronecker product A ⊗ B. Needed for multivariate LMMs (separable covariance via vec trick: vec(AXB) = (B' ⊗ A) vec(X)), tensor-structured models, and multi-output GP regression.

Scope:
- kron(A, B) returns the Kronecker product as adgeMatrix
- For m×n A and p×q B: result is mp×nq
- CPU path: base::kronecker() then rewrap
- MLX path: tile-based construction keeping result resident
- Register as S4 method on adgeMatrix pairs
- Tests: matches base::kronecker(), dimensions correct, kron(I_m, A) == block_diag(A,...) identity

title: Add mat_sqrt(), mat_pow(), mat_log() matrix functions via eigh
description:
Matrix functions K^p for symmetric positive (semi-)definite matrices. All three are one-liners given eigh() which is already implemented.

  mat_sqrt(K)      → Q diag(sqrt(λ)) Q'   whitening, preconditioning, Mahalanobis
  mat_pow(K, p)    → Q diag(λ^p) Q'       fractional powers, regularization paths
  mat_log(K)       → Q diag(log(λ)) Q'    information geometry, kernel log-det gradients

All accept adgeMatrix, dispatch eigh internally, return adgeMatrix.
Should warn/error when eigenvalues are negative (non-PSD input).
Tests: mat_sqrt(K) %*% mat_sqrt(K) ≈ K, mat_pow(K, 2) ≈ K %*% K, mat_log roundtrip.
close_reason: done

title: amatrix_calibrate(): profile-guided dispatch thresholds per machine
dense_lmm(): EMMA spectral LMM solver for dense covariance
description:
High-level model surface for linear mixed models where the random-effects covariance is a known dense SPD matrix K (genetic relatedness matrix, spatial kernel, etc.). Implements the EMMA/FaST-LMM spectral trick: decompose K once, reduce to 1D profile optimisation.

Depends on: amatrix-bg2 (am_eigh), amatrix-rrv (am_chol_logdet), amatrix-sdl (am_crossprod_weighted).

Algorithm:
1. am_eigh(K) -> Q, lambda  (GPU, done once)
2. Rotate: y* = Q'y, X* = Q'X  (GPU matmul)
3. Profile REML over delta = sigma2_e/sigma2_b via 1D Brent optimisation:
   - D(delta) = lambda + delta (diagonal)
   - beta(delta) = (X*' D^{-1} X*)^{-1} X*' D^{-1} y*
   - log-lik = -0.5 * (log|D| + (y* - X*beta)' D^{-1} (y* - X*beta))
4. Recover sigma2_e, sigma2_b, beta, BLUPs

API sketch:
  fit <- dense_lmm(y, X, K, backend='mlx', method='reml')
  fit$h2      # heritability / variance ratio
  fit$beta    # fixed effects
  fit$sigma2  # residual variance
  fit$loglik

Many-trait extension: dense_lmm(Y_many, X, K) reuses the single eigendecomposition across all traits — direct analogue of many_lm.

Target workloads: GWAS mixed model, spatial LMM, fMRI second-level with subject-level covariance.

New file: R/models-lmm.R
close_reason: Model surface belongs in a separate package. Core matrix primitives (am_eigh, am_chol_logdet, am_crossprod_weighted, am_xty_weighted, am_quad_form) are sufficient for an external package to implement efficient EMMA-style dense LMM. See am_rowscale/am_colscale issue for the one remaining gap.

title: Publish reproducible benchmark report for flagship workflows
description: DONE: Updated flagship-workflows.md with explicit 8.6x speedup table at 128 RHS, added Reproducing These Benchmarks section with runnable commands for all three flagship workflows, and a summary table mapping claims to scripts.

title: gemm transA+transB: fused GPU path via tcrossprod identity
notes: Currently materialises t(B) to host when both transA=TRUE and transB=TRUE. Fix: use t(B %*% A) tcrossprod identity or native transposed-matmul kernel. See R/wrappers.R gemm() comment and docs/milestones.json M7 known_gaps. Deferred from M7.

title: Run flagship benchmarks and write results doc
description: Run benchmark-chained-dense.R, benchmark-flagship-many-y.R, benchmark-svd-factor.R, benchmark-cholesky-runtime.R. Write docs/benchmark-results.md with speedup story: many_lm+cache (target 3-10x at n=4096), Cholesky chain (target 5-20x at n=2000), SVD factor (target 2-5x at n=2000). Honest: state machine spec, backend, precision. Closes amatrix-i5u.

title: Randomized SVD on ArrayFire (cross-backend rsvd parity)
close_reason: done

title: Decide product status of ArrayFire rsvd
description: DECISION (2026-04-06): experimental. Algorithm correct (standard rsvd with power iteration, corrected bridges from c21561d). Conformance test always skips — no real AF hardware in CI. No perf benchmark vs MLX. Recommended path on Apple Silicon is MLX. Documented in docs/backend-benchmarks.md and docs/next-steps.json.

title: ridge_path(X, Y, lambdas): full regularization path via single cached SVD
amatrix_explain(x, op): human-readable dispatch diagnostic with actionable suggestions
Design question: idiomatic R syntax for fused GEMM
description:
## Problem

We want users to write natural R and have amatrix transparently call a single fused GEMM (BLAS DGEMM: C ← α·A·B + β·C) instead of separate matmul + add operations. The question is how far we can get with pure S4 dispatch before we must either (a) expose an explicit am_gemm() function or (b) introduce deferred/lazy evaluation.

## What a fused GEMM buys

A single BLAS DGEMM call takes α, β, transA, transB, and an accumulator C in one round-trip. On GPU this matters because separate kernels mean separate dispatches and a read-modify-write cycle on C. MLX composes mlx_matmul + scaled mlx_add; ArrayFire has native af_gemm.

## Current state

- `%*%` dispatches via S4 setMethod to am_matmul() and returns an adgeMatrix immediately — no laziness.
- `t()` is setMethod("t", "adgeMatrix") → am_transpose() → **materializes to host, transposes in R, re-wraps** (wrappers.R:400). So t(A) %*% B currently incurs a full device→host→device round-trip before the matmul starts.
- No am_gemm() exists yet.

## Option A: Zero-copy t() + explicit am_gemm()

Fix t() to return a thin transposed_adgeMatrix subclass (a new S4 object with a transposed=TRUE slot, sharing the same device buffer). %*% dispatches on transposed_adgeMatrix → crossprod path, no copy.

For the accumulator case, expose am_gemm(A, B, C=NULL, alpha=1, beta=1, transA=FALSE, transB=FALSE) as an explicit power-user function.

User writes:

    t(A) %*% B          # → crossprod, zero copy, elegant
    am_gemm(A, B, C, alpha=2, beta=0.5, transA=TRUE)  # explicit for full signature

Tradeoffs:
- t() fix is independently correct and worth doing regardless
- am_gemm() is honest — users who need BLAS semantics ask for them explicitly
- am_gemm() is not idiomatic R; breaks the "looks like base R" goal

## Option B: scaled_adgeMatrix wrapper for alpha

Additionally intercept scalar multiplication: s * A returns a scaled_adgeMatrix(A, scalar=s). Then %*% on scaled_adgeMatrix extracts alpha before calling the backend.

User writes:

    2 * t(A) %*% B       # → am_matmul(A, B, alpha=2, transA=TRUE), no am_gemm needed

But beta/accumulator still requires am_gemm() or deferred eval, because by the time + is evaluated, the matmul result is already a plain adgeMatrix — there is no type-level signal that it was a matmul.

Tradeoffs:
- Handles alpha naturally
- 2 * A no longer returns an adgeMatrix; surprises users who pass it to other functions
- Adds a new public-ish class with coercion obligations
- Only covers alpha; beta still needs explicit am_gemm() for the full signature

## Option C: pending_matmul deferred class

%*% returns a lazy pending_matmul list. Arithmetic operators accumulate alpha/beta/C. Forced on as.matrix(), print(), or when passed to a non-amatrix consumer.

User writes:

    2 * t(A) %*% B + 0.5 * C   # → single am_gemm call at force time

This is the only option that captures the full GEMM signature from natural R.

Tradeoffs:
- Breaks the current S4 public contract: %*% is documented to return adgeMatrix; existing tests assert this
- adgeMatrix/adgCMatrix are S4 classes; pending_matmul is S3 — the dispatch model would need to bridge both
- t() as identity mutation is unsafe: object_id keys the residency registry and factor caches (QR, Cholesky, SVD). If t(A) shares identity with A, cached factor state silently corrupts
- Incomplete: print(), dim(), nrow(), Ops, and every generic that expects a matrix would need coercion guards
- "Passing to non-amatrix function forces evaluation" is correct in theory but hard to guarantee in practice

## The hard constraint

The beta/accumulator case (A %*% B + beta * C) cannot be fused without deferred evaluation. R evaluates %*% before + sees the result. At the point + is dispatched, both operands are plain adgeMatrix objects with no record of how they were produced. There is no pure-S4 mechanism to recover the matmul provenance.

## Key question for external input

Given:
1. The PRD commits to Matrix-compatible S4 semantics and "boring and eager-feeling" objects
2. The performance goal is to beat torch on shared-X many-Y workloads (not generic dense GEMM)
3. The accumulator pattern (beta*C) is rare in idiomatic R; alpha != 1 is also uncommon outside BLAS tutorials

Is the complexity of Option B or C justified, or is Option A (zero-copy t() + explicit am_gemm()) the right call? Is there a fourth option we have not considered?

title: Add batch_chol() and batch_solve() for parallel small-matrix factorizations
description:
Many statistical workflows require factorizing many small matrices in parallel rather than one large one: GWAS LD windows, bootstrap replicates, per-subject Cholesky factors, searchlight analyses. MLX supports batched BLAS natively (input shape [B, n, n]). Currently requires a slow R for-loop.

API:
  batch_chol(A)      # A is [B, n, n] array or list of n×n matrices → list/array of amChol
  batch_solve(L, B)  # L from batch_chol, B is [B, n, k] → [B, n, k] solutions
  batch_crossprod(A) # A is [B, n, p] → [B, p, p] array of X'X matrices

Backend:
- MLX: mlx_linalg_cholesky on batched array in one kernel call
- CPU fallback: lapply over list elements

Use case example (bootstrap):
  Ks <- array(dim=c(B, n, n))  # B bootstrap kernel matrices
  Ls <- batch_chol(Ks)
  alphas <- batch_solve(Ls, Y_rep)  # solve all B systems at once
close_reason: done

title: Investigate MLX GPU stream for QR factorization
description: FINDING: MLX 0.31.1 does not support QR on GPU stream — raises ValueError: [linalg::qr] This op is not yet supported on the GPU. The current mlx_default_cpu_stream_new() usage in amatrix_mlx_qr_real and amatrix_mlx_tsqr_build_real is forced-correct, not a bug. Revisit when MLX adds GPU QR support.

title: Expose public am_gemm() from the current internal .am_gemm scaffold
description: DONE: renamed .am_gemm to am_gemm, fixed transA&&transB case to use am_crossprod(A, am_transpose(B)) for correct t(A)%*%t(B) semantics, exported in NAMESPACE, 18 tests passing.

title: Add rowmeans() and colmeans() row/column mean reductions
description:
Direct parallel to existing rowsums/colsums. Currently missing despite being one of the most common matrix operations. Required for centering, normalization, and any preprocessing pipeline that avoids breaking GPU residency via apply().

Scope:
- rowmeans(X, na.rm=FALSE) — row means, returns vector or adgeMatrix
- colmeans(X, na.rm=FALSE) — column means, returns vector or adgeMatrix
- Register setMethod rowMeans/colMeans on adgeMatrix and adgCMatrix (S4 dispatch like rowSums/colSums)
- CPU fallback via base::rowMeans/colMeans
- Tests: matches base R, dimensions correct, na.rm respected
close_reason: rowmeans/colmeans added in R/wrappers.R with S4 dispatch in methods-dense.R and methods-sparse.R. 957 tests pass.

title: Add am_chol_logdet() log-determinant from amChol factor
description:
Every REML log-likelihood evaluation needs log|V| = 2 * sum(log(diag(L))). Currently users must reach into factor@factor manually. This trivial omission blocks writing clean REML objectives on top of amatrix.

Scope:
- Add am_chol_logdet(factor) to R/chol-factor.R alongside am_chol_solve()
- Export in NAMESPACE
- Accepts an amChol object, returns a scalar double
- Also consider am_chol_diag(factor) returning diag(L) as a convenience
- Tests: logdet matches log(det(K)) for small SPD matrices, matches 2*sum(log(diag(chol(K))))
close_reason: Implemented am_chol_logdet, am_chol_diag in R/chol-factor.R. 18 new tests pass (887 total).

title: Add solve_triangular(L, B, lower=TRUE) explicit triangular solve
description:
Explicit trsm-level triangular solve. Currently triangular solves are only accessible through chol_solve (which requires an amChol wrapper). Callers who already have a raw upper/lower triangular factor R (e.g., from qr.R(), a custom factorization, or the factor slot of amChol) cannot access the fast triangular solve path directly.

API:
  solve_triangular(R, B, lower = FALSE)  # upper triangular by default (Cholesky convention)
  solve_triangular(L, B, lower = TRUE)   # lower triangular

Backend:
  CPU: backsolve() / forwardsolve()
  MLX: mlx triangular solve bridge (same path as chol_solve internals)

Tests: solve_triangular(R, R %*% x) ≈ x for known x, lower=TRUE variant,
  multiple RHS columns.
close_reason: done

title: Add am_crossprod_weighted(X, w) fused weighted normal equations
description:
Computes X'WX where W = diag(w) without materializing the n×n diagonal or the n×p intermediate. Currently wls_fit does x_host * sqrt_w then crossprod, allocating a full copy. For large n (neuroimaging, GWAS) this dominates memory bandwidth. This is the critical primitive for PIRLS-style iterations in dense LMMs.

Scope:
- am_crossprod_weighted(X, w) — X' diag(w) X, returns p×p matrix
- am_tcrossprod_weighted(X, w) — X diag(w) X', returns n×n matrix (use with care for large n)
- am_xty_weighted(X, w, y) — X' diag(w) y, returns p×k matrix (weighted cross-moment)
- Backend: MLX can fuse via element-wise scale then matmul in one resident pass; CPU fallback is the sqrt-scale trick
- Migrate wls_fit internals to use this path
- Tests: matches manual diag(w) %*% X path, wls_fit results unchanged
close_reason: Implemented am_crossprod_weighted(X,w) and am_xty_weighted(X,w,y) in R/wrappers.R using sqrt-scale trick. 18 new tests pass.

title: Fix benchmark units and add warm-path variant
description: median_ms column is likely µs (bench_time ns × 1e3). Verify and fix label. Add warm variant that pre-uploads before timing to show real GPU compute speedup. Files: tools/benchmark-regression.R. Acceptance: correct units; warm path shows >2x MLX at medium/large sizes for matmul/crossprod; baseline.csv updated.

title: Tiled am_dist for large n
description: am_dist(X, tile_size=N) for n>50k; auto-tiles when nrow(X)>50000. Implemented in R/wrappers.R.
close_reason: Implemented .am_dist_tiled() in R/wrappers.R; am_dist() gains tile_size param; auto-tiles for n>50k; 6 new tests all pass (tiled matches untiled within 1e-6, symmetric, diagonal=0).

title: Add sym(A) symmetry enforcement (A + t(A)) / 2
description:
Enforces symmetry via (A + t(A)) / 2. After floating-point accumulation in crossprod or Cholesky paths, SPD matrices drift asymmetric. Standard fix before chol_factor() on a computed matrix.

Scope:
- sym(A) returns (A + t(A)) / 2 as adgeMatrix preserving backend/precision
- GPU path: keep on device without host round-trip
- Tests: result is exactly symmetric, matches manual (A+t(A))/2, idempotent sym(sym(A))==sym(A)
close_reason: sym(A) added in R/wrappers.R as (A + t(A))/2, idempotent, preserves backend/precision. 957 tests pass.

title: Add norm(X, type) matrix and vector norms with GPU dispatch
description:
S4 setMethod on adgeMatrix for base R norm(). GPU-dispatched norms avoid materializing large matrices for what is essentially a reduction.

Types to support:
- 'F' / 'f': Frobenius norm = sqrt(sum(X^2)) — GPU-friendly reduction
- '1': max column sum (max of colsums of abs values)
- 'I': max row sum (max of rowsums of abs values)
- '2': largest singular value (expensive; dispatch to rsvd for large matrices)
- default: same as base R norm()

Scope:
- Register setMethod('norm', 'adgeMatrix', ...)
- Frobenius and 1/I norms via resident rowsums/colsums paths
- CPU fallback for '2' norm below rsvd threshold
- Tests: matches base::norm() for all types on both small and large matrices

title: KronMatrix: structured Kronecker A⊗B class avoiding O(p^2 q^2) materialization
Add crossprod_add_diag(X, lambda) fused X'X + lambda*I
description:
Fused computation of X'X + lambda*I without materializing X'X as an intermediate. Critical hot path for ridge regression, kernel ridge, GP posterior, and any regularized normal equations. Currently requires two ops and a host round-trip.

API:
  crossprod_add_diag(X, lambda)     # X'X + lambda * I  (p×p result)
  crossprod_add_diag(X, d)          # X'X + diag(d) when d is a vector (diagonal regularization)

Backend:
  GPU: compute crossprod then fuse diagonal add in one resident pass
  CPU fallback: crossprod(X) + lambda * diag(ncol(X))

Common pattern:
  L <- chol_factor(crossprod_add_diag(X, lambda))  # ridge in one line
close_reason: done

title: amatrix_warm(): pre-compile GPU kernels to eliminate cold-start latency
amatrix_memory_stats(): GPU memory usage and budget API per backend
